coredata.dc:title: Fast photogenerated electron transfer in N-GQDs/PTI/ZnO-QDs ternary heterostructured nanosheets for photocatalytic H2 evolution under visible light
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Hui
Cao, Yuqi
Zhong, Lei
Cao, Xin
He, Jinghui
Sun, Jianhua
Lei, Weiwei
coredata.dc:description: The efficiency of photogenerated charge transport is one of the critical factors determining the photocatalytic performance in heterostructured photocatalysts. In this work, a two-dimensional conjugated polytriazine imide (PTI)-based photocatalyst was developed to achieve fast photoexcited electron transfer. These heterostructured nanosheets were synthesized by a one-pot condensation of a precursor mixture of cyanamide and N-doped graphene quantum dots (N-GQDs) in ZnCl2 containing salt melts. The ionothermal synthetic approach results in the N-GQDs/PTI in-plane heterostructured nanosheets, loaded with well-dispersed ZnO quantum dots (ZnO-QDs). Especially, the N-GQDs are coupled with PTI through the nitrogen atoms at the edge of triazine rings, forming new π-p-π conjugated connection units. Therefore, the obtained ternary heterostructured nanosheets can synchronously promote photoelectron transport through the new π-p-π conjugated networks as well as extend the optical response. Importantly, it exhibits approximately 230 and 3.6 times enhancement of H2 evolution over pure PTI and the PTI/ZnO-QDs heterostructure under visible light irradiation, respectively.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Polytriazine imide
N-doped graphene quantum dots
In-plane heterostructure
Photocatalytic hydrogen evolution

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of PTI and PTI-based composites

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM and HRTEM images of NPZ0.5 (a and b) and N-GQDs/PTI (c and d). The inset shows an enlarged image marked in (b).
(a) UV–vis DRS and (b) photoluminescence spectra of NPZx (x = 0, 0.2, 0.5, 1, 2) samples. The insert is the enlarged UV–vis DRS of NPZ0 and NPZ0.5.
XPS spectra of NPZ0 and NPZ0.5. (a) high-resolution C 1s spectra, (b) high-resolution N 1s spectra.

(a) Stability test of NPZ0.5 for H2 production under visible light irradiation (λ ≥ 420 nm) and (b) wavelength-dependent AQE of H2 evolution over NPZ0.5 (right axis), and UV–vis light absorption spectrum of NPZ0.5 (left axis).
(a) EIS Nyquist plots and (b) transient photocurrent responses of NPZ0, NPZ0.5 and NPZ2.
Proposed mechanism for the photocatalytic H2 evolution by NPZ under visible light irradiation.
Schematic illustration for the preparation of NPZ ternary composites.
Surface areas, physicochemical properties and photocatalytic activities of different catalysts for hydrogen evolution under visible light.

head.ce:title: Fast photogenerated electron transfer in N-GQDs/PTI/ZnO-QDs ternary heterostructured nanosheets for photocatalytic H2 evolution under visible light

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Environmental Engineering, Institute of Advanced Functional Materials for Energy, Jiangsu University of Technology, Changzhou 213001, Jiangsu Province, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Environmental Engineering
Institute of Advanced Functional Materials for Energy

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry, Chemical Engineering and Materials Science, Collaborative Innovation Center of Suzhou Nano Science and Technology, Soochow University, Suzhou 215123, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Collaborative Innovation Center of Suzhou Nano Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute for Frontier Materials, Deakin University, Waurn Ponds, Victoria 3216, Australia

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The efficiency of photogenerated charge transport is one of the critical factors determining the photocatalytic performance in heterostructured photocatalysts. In this work, a two-dimensional conjugated polytriazine imide (PTI)-based photocatalyst was developed to achieve fast photoexcited electron transfer. These heterostructured nanosheets were synthesized by a one-pot condensation of a precursor mixture of cyanamide and N-doped graphene quantum dots (N-GQDs) in ZnCl2 containing salt melts. The ionothermal synthetic approach results in the N-GQDs/PTI in-plane heterostructured nanosheets, loaded with well-dispersed ZnO quantum dots (ZnO-QDs). Especially, the N-GQDs are coupled with PTI through the nitrogen atoms at the edge of triazine rings, forming new π-p-π conjugated connection units. Therefore, the obtained ternary heterostructured nanosheets can synchronously promote photoelectron transport through the new π-p-π conjugated networks as well as extend the optical response. Importantly, it exhibits approximately 230 and 3.6 times enhancement of H2 evolution over pure PTI and the PTI/ZnO-QDs heterostructure under visible light irradiation, respectively.

ce:section.ce:para: The photocatalytic production of hydrogen from water splitting is a promising strategy for converting solar energy to chemical energy in the form of clean and renewable fuel. To achieve this goal, semiconductor photocatalysts have been widely studied over past few decades [1–3]. As is well known, the photocatalytic reaction primarily involves three processes: photoabsorption and electron-hole pairs generation; photocarrier separation and migration; and surface reduction/oxidation reactions. Unfortunately, it is difficult to balance all the above terms simultaneously on a single semiconductor photocatalyst [4]. To overcome this issue, heterostructured photocatalysts stacked by various materials such as CdS/TiO2 [5,6] and MoS2/graphene [7–9] have been sought to tune the band structure and restrain the photo-generated charge recombination. However, most of these photocatalysts are van der Waals heterostructures. It is still a great obstacle of sluggish photocarrier transfer because the interfacial transfer resistance can lead to the limited increase of photocarrier separation. Thus, developing novel heterostructures with more efficient interfacial charge transfer is highly imperative.

In recent years, polymeric semiconductor carbon nitride (CN) with favorable band gap has emerged as an effective photocatalyst for water splitting [10–14]. Owing to the two-dimensional conjugated unites and tunable electronic structures, it shows great promise for fast photocarrier transfer. The basic subunit of CN would be either triazine or heptazine or even both, interconnected through NH-bridges, forming π-conjugated networks. A well-defined and crystalline CN, polytriazine imide (PTI) has been synthesized in molten salts [15–17]. Recently, we reported on the one-step synthesis of PTI/ZnO quantum dots (PTI/ZnO-QDs) heterostructure composites in the KCl/ZnCl2 salt melts [18]. This binary heterojunction presents a significantly improved photocatalytic activity for H2 evolution with respect to pure PTI. Even so, the transport efficiency of photogenerated electrons is still not satisfied due to the low electrical conductivity and the obvious interfacial resistance. To accelerate the charge carrier transfer, it is necessary to introduce a new electron mediator between PTI and ZnO QDs.

Carbonaceous candidates such as carbon quantum dots (CQDs) and graphene quantum dots (GQDs) have been used as solid-state electron mediator for the construction of heterostructured photocatalysts [19–24]. However, in most of the reported heterostructures, the intermolecular forces between the carbon dots and the semiconductors are usually relatively weak. They predominantly interact via physisorption, electrostatic attraction or van der Waals forces. Therefore, they may not be beneficial for the fast interfacial charge transfer. To end this, a strong electronic coupling or a covalent bond instead of the weak intermolecular interaction would be considerable.

Compared with GQDs, N-doped graphene quantum dots (N-GQDs) decorated with abundant amino groups [25,26] could be an ideal precursor, which can be stitched with PTI via NC bonds by thermal condensation [27]. Besides, the coupling of N-GQDs may promote the π-electron delocalization [28,29], and provide new transfer paths for light with lower energy and thus facilitate visible light harvesting.

Herein, to achieve more effective interfacial charge transfer, we introduced N-GQDs as the solid-state electron mediator into the PTI/ZnO-QDs heterostructure. New N-GQDs/PTI/ZnO-QDs (NPZ) ternary heterostructure composites were developed by a thermal pyrolysis of pre-synthesized N-GQDs and cyanamide in ZnCl2-based salt melts (As shown in Scheme 1 ). In the synthesis, ZnCl2 acts not only as the reactive solvent but also as the precursor for the formation of ZnO QDs. During the polymerization process, N-GQDs with abundant amino as the leaving groups could bond to the s-triazine units through the nitrogen atoms with rich lone-pair electrons at the edge of triazine rings, forming new π-p-π conjugated connection units. In addition, the ZnO QDs are dispersed on the N-GQDs−nitrogen−PTI nanosheets very well. It is believed that the enhancement of photocatalytic H2-evolution of NPZ sample is due to the fast photogenerated electron transfer and extended optical response resulting from the construction of the new in-plane π-p-π conjugated networks.

N-GQDs were prepared according to the method reported in the previous literature [26]. Typically, 0.72 g (12 mmol) urea and 0.84 g (4.0 mmol) citric acid were dissolved in 20 mL water. The obtained solution was then transferred into a 50 mL Teflon lined stainless autoclave and heated in an electric oven at 160 °C for 4 h. After cooling to the room temperature, 200 mL ethanol was added to the resulting solution. The precipitant was collected as the product by centrifugation and dried at 80 °C. The product was then re-dispersed into water to afford a dispersion of 5.0 mg·mL−1.

Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) were synthesized by the pyrolysis of citric acid as reported in the literature [30]. Typically, 2.0 g citric acid was heated by a heating mantle to ca. 200 °C and kept for 30 min. An orange liquid was obtained and then was dialyzed in water by a MWCO1000 membrane. The dialyzed solution was lyophilized to afford the GQDs powder.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of PTI and PTI-based composites
ce:section.ce:para: N-GQDs/PTI/ZnO-QDs composites were prepared by a molten salt method. 2.0 g cyanamide and a certain volume of 5.0 mg·mL−1 as prepared N-GQDs dispersion (0, 0.2, 0.5, 1.0 and 2.0 mL) were thoroughly grounded together with 20 g of a mixture of potassium chloride and zinc chloride (48: 52 wt%). The reaction mixture was transferred into a porcelain crucible and heated in a muffle furnace at a ramp of 2.1 °C min−1 to 520 °C. The temperature was held for 4 h, and the sample was then cooled to room temperature. After washed with abundant boiling water to remove residual salts, the solid was collected as the product and dried at 60 °C. The obtained samples were labelled as NPZx (x = 0, 0.2, 0.5, 1, 2), where x refers to the initial volume of N-GQDs dispersion. Note that PTI/ZnO-QDs sample, which was prepared in the absence of N-GQDs, was simply denoted as NPZ0.

PTI was synthesized according to the procedure in the reported literature [18]. Typically, 0.50 g PTI/ZnO-QDs (NPZ0) was added to 200 mL 2 M HCl solution. The obtained dispersion was stirred for 1 day at room temperature. After that, the solid was thoroughly washed with water and dried at 60 °C. The resulting white solid was heated in a muffle furnace at 350 °C for 1 h to completely remove HCl. Final product was obtained as a yellow powder.

N-GQDs/PTI composite was synthesized as follows: 0.50 g NPZ0.5 was added to 200 mL 2 M HCl solution. The obtained dispersion was stirred at room temperature for 1 day. After that, the solid was thoroughly washed with water and dried at 60 °C. The obtained light brown solid was transferred to a covered porcelain crucible and placed in a tube furnace, heated under N2 at 350 °C for 1 h to completely remove HCl. Final product was obtained as a brown powder.
GQDs/PTI/ZnO-QDs (GPZ) composite was prepared following the same procedure as that of NPZ0.5 except that a dispersion of GQDs was used instead of N-GQDs dispersion.

Physically mixed N-GQDs+NPZ0 sample was prepared as follows: 0.40 g NPZ0 was dispersed into 10 mL ethanol. After addition of 0.40 mL N-GQDs dispersion (5.0 mg mL−1), the mixture was stirred at 80 °C to remove ethanol. The resulting solid was collected and dried at oven to obtain the final sample.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) images were obtained on a JEM-2100 microscopy. Powder X-ray diffraction (XRD) characterizations were measured on a PANalytical diffractometer Model PW3040/60 X'pert PRO with Cu Kα radiation (λ = 0.1548 nm). Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) as well as the energy dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDX) analyses were recorded on a ZEISS Sigma 500 attached an Oxford Instruments X-MaxN 50 energy-dispersive spectrometer. Nitrogen adsorption-desorption isotherms were performed on a Micromeritics ASAP 2020 nitrogen adsorption apparatus. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) measurements were performed on a VG Multilab 2000 X-ray photoelectron spectrometer using an Al Kα radiation excitation source. The UV–vis diffuse reflection spectra (DRS) were taken on a Shimadzu UV-2600 spectrophotometer using BaSO4 as the reference. Photoluminescent (PL) emission spectra were recorded on a Cary Eclipse fluorescence spectrometer and the excitation wavelength was 350 nm.

The photocatalytic activities of the as-prepared samples were evaluated by the H2 evolution reactions carried out in a top-irradiation reaction vessel. 0.05 g of the powder samples were dispersed in 100 mL aqueous solution containing 10 mL triethanolamine (TEOA) as sacrificial agent. Pt (3 wt%) cocatalyst was photodeposited onto the surface of the samples using H2PtCl6 as the precursor. Prior to the irradiation under a 300 W Xe lamp with a cut-off filter (λ ≥ 420 nm), the reactant solution was degassed with vacuum. The temperature was controlled at 20 °C by cycle water and the evolved gases were analyzed by gas chromatography using argon as the carrier gas. The apparent quantum efficiency (AQE) for H2 evolution was measured by replacing the cut-off filter with a λ 0 ± 15 nm band-pass filter. The AQE is calculated from the following equation:
AQE =
2 × number of evolved
H 2
molecules
the number of incident photos
× 100 %

The photoelectrochemical measurements were carried out in a standard three-electrode system by a Bio-Logic SP-150 workstation. The prepared samples, an Ag/AgCl electrode and a Pt sheet were used as the working electrodes, the reference electrode and the counter electrode, respectively. For the electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) measurements, a 0.1 M KCl aqueous solution containing 5.0 mM K3[Fe(CN)6]/K4[Fe(CN)6] (1:1) was used as the electrolyte. And for the photocurrent measurements, a 0.5 M Na2SO4 aqueous solution was used. The working electrodes were prepared as follows: 5 mg sample was dispersed into 1.00 mL 0.02 wt% Nafion solution. The obtained suspension was sonicated for 1 h and then spread on to a 1.0 cm × 1.0 cm exposed ITO glass. After dried in air, the working electrode was further dried at 80 °C for 2 h.

The NPZ samples were synthesized as illustrated in Scheme 1. Similar to the pyrolysis of cyanamide, N-GQDs rich in amino groups could connect to the cyanamide-drived PTI through NC bonds, resulting in the N-GQDs/PTI nanosheets [27]. Simultaneously formed ZnO QDs randomly dispersed on the nanosheets, which can be verified by TEM and HRTEM. As shown in Fig. 1a and S1a, the NPZ0.5 sample is composed of nanosheets, which is similar to the NPZ0 sample synthesized in the absence of N-GQDs. Further, as shown in Fig. 1b, ZnO QDs (3–4 nm) with a (002) crystal lattice spacing of ~0.26 nm [31] are well-dispersed on the surface of N-GQDs/PTI sheet in NPZ0.5 sample. As ZnO QDs are randomly dispersed on the N-GQDs containing nanosheets, a part of them could connect to the N-GQDs. In this case, N-GQDs serve as bridges connecting PTI and Zn-QDs. However, the presence of crystalline ZnO makes N-GQDs difficult to observe clearly from TEM directly. In order to confirm the existence of N-GQDs in NPZ0.5, the sample was washed with 2 M HCl to remove ZnO, and the TEM and HRTEM images of the obtained sample, namely N-GQDs/PTI, are shown in Fig. 1c and d. As expected, the homogeneous N-GQDs/PTI sheet shows a relative smooth surface without any obvious nanoparticles. Clear lattice structures belonging to N-GQDs are recognized in the HRTEM image of N-GQDs/PTI, which are similar to those of the N-GQDs precursor shown in Fig. S2b.

Nitrogen adsorption-desorption isotherms of all the as-prepared samples are present in Fig. S3. Typical type-IV isotherms featuring H3-type hysteresis loops are observed for NPZ0, NPZ0.2, NPZ0.5 and NPZ1, and a typical type-IV isotherm with a pronounced H4-type hysteresis loop is observed for NPZ2. The calculated BET surface areas are displayed in Table 1 . As listed in Table 1, the surface areas of NPZ0, NPZ0.2 and NPZ0.5 increase with enhanced N-GQDs concentrations. When the N-GQDs content is further increased, a decrease of the surface areas occurs. Especially, a much smaller specific surface area of NPZ2 sample is observed, which is probably due to the disturbance of PTI structure by the excess N-GQDs. Moreover, elemental analysis shows that the molecular ratios of C/N are similar for all the samples except NPZ2.

The XRD patterns of all the prepared samples are shown in Fig. 2 . The NPZ0 sample exhibits three distinct XRD peaks which can be identified according to the crystal phase of the reported PTI [15,32]. The peaks at 2θ = 12.1°, 21.0° and 32.3° can be assigned to (100), (110) and (210) plane of PTI, respectively. These three characteristic peaks of NPZ0 are consistent with our previous research [18], suggesting the successful fabrication of the PTI/ZnO-QDs heterojunction. With increase amount of N-GQDs, the intensities of all the three peaks decrease gradually. It indicates a strong interaction coupling between PTI and N-GQDs, most probably a new formed NC covalent bond in the NPZ system.

To demonstrate the above hypothesis, the UV–vis DRS and photoluminescence (PL) spectra of the as-prepared samples were measured and displayed in Fig. 3 . The UV–vis DRS in Fig. 3a show that with the increasing content of N-GQDs, the absorptions of the composites are remarkably extended in the visible region. Moreover, the π → π*absorption bands of the NPZ0.2, NPZ0.5, NPZ1 and NPZ2 samples in UV light regions are obviously redshifted compared to that of NPZ0, indicating the N-GQDs strongly interact with PTI and efficiently extend the conjugated π-electron system. It is noteworthy that the coupling of N-GQDs results in a new absorption band around 480 nm. The insert illustrates the enlarged UV–vis DRS of NPZ0.5 sample compared to that of NPZ0. The new absorption band of NPZ0.5 can arise from the n → π* transition [33], likely from the nitrogen atom which link the N-GQDs and PTI to the triazine ring. This new absorption band is a direct evidence that there is a new NC bond between N-GQDs and PTI, leading to the creation of in-plane π-p-π network.

To further support the deduction, non‑nitrogen graphene quantum dots (GQDs) were used as the precursor instead of N-GQDs to prepare the GQDs/PTI/ZnO-QDs (GPZ) composite for comparison. On account of the absence of amino groups, GQDs failed to form the NC bonds with PTI during the thermal condensation. In the UV–vis DRS of the GPZ sample (Fig. S4), no obvious n → π* transition band can be observed, indicating the amino groups in N-GQDs is crucial to forming the π-p-π network. Moreover, the band around 480 nm is absent in the UV–vis DRS of the physically mixed N-GQDs+NPZ0 sample, also confirmed this band is attribute to the new formed π-p-π network, not N-GQDs nor PTI.

All the as-prepared samples are emissive in the solid state. The PL emission spectra of NPZ samples are shown in Fig. 3b. The NPZ0 sample exhibits a relatively strong fluorescence emission peak at 410 nm, which is ascribed to the band-band transition in PTI. With increasing amount of N-GQDs, a dramatic emission quenching is observed for NPZx samples, indicating the suppressed recombination of photogenerated charge carriers due to the incorporation of N-GQDs. Meanwhile, a new emission peak at 485 nm appears. It strongly verifies the in-plane coupling of N-GQDs and triazine units, which creates new intermediate energy levels in the band gap, providing novel transfer paths for visible light with lower energy [34]. As a result, the optical response of the NPZ system is remarkably extended in the visible light region.

We also investigated the emission spectra of GPZ and N-GQDs+NPZ0 sample. As shown in Fig. S5, an obvious emission at 485 nm is present in the spectrum of NPZ0.5, but is absent in the spectra of GPZ and N-GQDs+NPZ0 samples. Considering the similar structure and photophysical properties of GQDs and N-GQDs, it is deduced that the N-GQDs bond to the s-triazine units through the nitrogen atoms at the edge of triazine rings to construct an in-plane heterostructure in the NPZ composites. While the GQDs without amino groups could only stack on the surface of the PTI to form a van der Waals heterojunction in the GPZ composite. For the physically mixed N-GQDs+NPZ0 sample, the N-GQDs also stack on the surface of PTI, thus presents a similar emission spectrum with respect to that of GPZ. Therefore, we conclude that the emissions at 485 nm of NPZ composites are originated from the new formed π-p-π network between N-GQDs and PTI.

XPS was performed to evaluate the surface chemical composition and intimate interaction between N-GQDs and PTI. For the NPZ0 and NPZ0.5 samples, the presence of C, N, O and Zn elements were confirmed, as shown in Fig. S6, in line with the results of EDX analysis (Fig. S7). From the high-resolution C 1s spectra (Fig. 4a), two major peaks are identified for NPZ0 and NPZ0.5, one at 284.6 eV attributed to graphitic carbon (C=C) and another at 287.9 eV assigned to the sp2-hybridized carbon in the triazine ring (N–C=N) [35]. It is worth noting that the peak at 284.6 eV (31.9%) of the sample NPZ0.5 are stronger than that of NPZ0 (28.3%), implying that the NPZ0.5 possesses more CC groups. The increased CC groups in NPZ0.5 indicate that the N-GQDs have been incorporated into the composite. The high-resolution N 1s spectra are shown in Fig. 4b. There are mainly two nitrogen species presented in the N 1s spectrum for NPZ0: one (398.3 eV) is attributed to the sp2 N atoms in the triazine rings and the other one (399.3 eV) is regarded as bridging N atoms in NH [16,36]. The extra peak at 398.8 eV in NPZ0.5 can be assigned to the graphitic N in N-GQDs, further verifying the incorporation of N-GQDs. In addition, the decreased peak at 399.3 eV (29.4%) of NPZ0.5 compared to that of NPZ0 (44.8%) indicates that there are less NH groups in NPZ0.5. It confirms the connection through the bridging N atoms between heterogeneous domains, as illustrated in Scheme 1. Therefore, the incorporation of N-GQDs has a significant influence on the structure of PTI, which may affect its photocatalytic activities.

Visible-light-induced H2 evolution was conducted to evaluate the photocatalytic performance of these samples. As listed in Table 1, the photocatalytic activities of NPZ samples are sensitive to the concentration of N-GQDs in the composites. Among all the as-prepared samples, the initial H2 evolution rate for NPZ0.5 is observed to be the highest, 68 μmol·h−1, which is ca. 230 times higher than that of pure PTI (0.3 μmol·h−1) and 3.6 times higher than that of NPZ0 (19 μmol·h−1). In consideration of similar surface areas between NPZ0.5 and NPZ0, the effect of photocarrier transfer kinetics could be excluded. When the N-GQDs content is further increased, an obvious drop of the H2 evolution rate occurs even though the optical absorption is greatly improved. It suggests that a subtle amount of N-GQDs can promote the photocatalytic activity of the composites, while an excess of N-GQDs breaks the crystal structure of PTI and is not favorable for the photocatalytic water-splitting.

In addition, the photocatalytic stability of the NPZ composite was also investigated. As shown in Fig. 5a, the NPZ0.5 sample exhibits a good stability and maintains a high photocatalytic activity within 12 h, suggesting that the NPZ composite is stable for photocatalytic H2 evolution. The apparent quantum efficiency (AQE) for NPZ0.5 is calculated to be 3.37% at λ = 420 nm, and could approximatively match the light absorption behavior from 400 to 650 nm (Fig. 5b).

For better understanding of the effect of N-GQDs on the photoinduced charge transport and separation in the NPZ system, EIS and transient photocurrent responses of NPZ0, NPZ0.5 and NPZ2 samples were carried out. As shown in Fig. 6a, the semicircle of NPZ0.5 is smaller than that of NPZ0. This suggests that a more efficient charge transport has been obtained by incorporating of N-GQDs. Nevertheless, when excess N-GQDs were introduced into the system, the structure of PTI would be distorted and an enhanced charge transfer resistance is observed for NPZ2. On the other hand, the photocurrent density generated from NPZ0.5 is higher than those of NPZ0 and NPZ2 (Fig. 6b), further confirming efficient transport and separation of electron-hole pairs in NPZ0.5. The increase of photocurrent of NPZ0.5 is also attributed to the enhanced optical response in the N-GQDs−nitrogen−PTI (π-p-π) architecture.

Summarizing all the above characterization and discussion, the NPZ ternary heterostructure is a promising photocatalyst for H2 evolution. Under visible-light irradiation, the electrons in the valence band (VB) of PTI can be excited and injected into its conduction band (CB) to drive the H2 evolution reaction, leaving the holes in the VB for TEOA oxidation. However, the photogenerated electron-hole pairs will quickly recombine in pure PTI, thus leading to a low photocatalytic activity. Our previous studies have demonstrated that coupling with ZnO QDs results in the formation of a PTI/ZnO heterostructure promoting charge separation and enhancing H2 generation [18]. Unfortunately, the low electrical conductivity, obvious interfacial resistance and poor absorption of visible light of PTI/ZnO heterostructure are unfavorable for the photocatalytic reaction. When the N-GQDs are introduced into the system, the photocatalytic H2-evolution activity is further improved. In this case, the N-GQDs link to the s-triazine units and play critical roles in suppression of photocarrier recombination and enhancement of visible-light utilization. Therefore, a possible mechanism for photocatalytic H2 evolution is proposed (Fig. 7 ). Firstly, the in-plane coupling of N-GQDs and PTI forms a two-dimensional π-p-π network, which would allow fast and efficient charge transfer. Due to the excellent electronic conductivity, the N-GQDs could serve as interfacial bridges between PTI and ZnO QDs for accelerating the transfer of photogenerated electrons and thus significantly suppress the electron-hole recombination [22–24]. This could be supported by the EIS and transient photocurrent responses of the samples. Secondly, the strong coupling of N-GQDs and PTI creates new intermediate energy levels in the band gap, providing novel transfer paths for visible light with lower energy, thus extends the optical response of the NPZ system. This could be verified by UV–vis DRS and PL spectra. Finally, the enriched mobile electrons together with the improved electrical conductivity modify the surface property of NPZ, which can accelerate the photocatalytic H2 evolution on the ZnO QDs.

In this study, N-GQDs have been introduced into PTI/ZnO heterojunction via a one-pot approach to prepare N-GQDs/PTI/ZnO-QDs ternary heterostructure nanosheets in ZnCl2-based salt melts. N-GQDs and PTI are conjugated linked through the nitrogen atoms at the edge of triazine rings, forming a new in-plane π-p-π network, which allows fast and efficient charge transfer. In addition, the in-situ synthesized ZnO QDs are well-dispersed on the PTI-based nanosheets enabling an excellent interfacial contact. The resulting NPZ0.5 heterostructure exhibits an excellent photocatalytic activity under visible light irradiation with H2 production rate up to 68 μmol·h−1, which is about 230 higher than that of the pure PTI and 3.6 times higher than that of the PTI/ZnO composite. It is believed that the enhanced photocatalytic activity can be attributed to the combined effects of the improved charge transfer and separation, extended optical response and accelerated H2-evolution kinetics. This work provides a feasible strategy to fabricate ternary PTI-based heterostructures, and may propose a new mechanistic insight into the development of multi-heterostructured composite photocatalysts for H2 evolution.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21373103 and 51702134), the Natural Science Foundation of Jiangsu Province (No. BK20170310 and BK20170316), and the Australian Research Council Discovery Early Career Researcher Award scheme (DE140100716).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.apsusc.2019.04.230.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Electrochemical photolysis of water at a semiconductor electrode
Recent advances in semiconductors for photocatalytic and photoelectrochemical water splitting
State-of-the-art progress in diverse heterostructured photocatalysts toward promoting photocatalytic performance
Effect of quantum dot deposition on the interfacial flatband potential, depletion layer in TiO2 nanotube electrodes, and resulting H2 generation rates
Construction of TiO2/CdS heterojunction photocatslysts with enhanced visible light activity
Catalysis with two-dimensional materials and their heterostructures
